title: Power Budgeting in Series Circuits
learningObjectives: Calculate total power from voltage and current
See how higher resistance reduces power draw
Tune voltage and resistance to stay within safe limits
introText: Power tells you how much energy a circuit consumes. With a single loop you can keep parts cool by raising resistance or lowering the supply voltage.

steps.instructionText: Add a 12V source and a resistor, then wire them into a closed series loop.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Closed loop built. Current can flow and you can measure total power safely.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Wire the source and resistor together end-to-end so the loop is closed.

steps.instructionText: Set the resistor near 600Ω. Check that the total power stays under 0.3 W.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Great — power is below 0.3 W. The resistor isn't overheating.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Increase resistance until total power drops under 0.3 W. Higher resistance lowers both current and power.

steps.instructionText: Lower the source to about 6V to cut power even further.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Excellent — with half the voltage, power is under 0.1 W. Power scales with both voltage and current.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Drop the source voltage. At the same resistance, lower voltage slashes the power used.

questions.questionText: How is total power calculated in this circuit model?
questions.explanation: Power is the product of voltage and current (P = V × I).

questions.questionText: What happens to power if you cut the supply voltage in half while keeping resistance the same?
questions.explanation: Halving voltage halves current in series, so power (V × I) drops to one quarter.
